subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Sequences & Series
stimulus: Let $f$ be the function defined by $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{2x^2 - 7x + 5}$.

parts.prompt: Find the slope of the line tangent to the graph of $f$ at $x = 3$.

scoring_points.description: Differentiates $f(x) = (2x^2 - 7x + 5)^{-1}$ correctly using the chain rule

parts.prompt: Find the $x$-coordinate of each critical point of $f$ in the interval $1 < x < 2.5$. Classify each critical point as the location of a relative minimum, a relative maximum, or neither. Justify your answers.

scoring_points.description: Sets $f'(x) = 0$: finds $4x - 7 = 0$, so $x = \frac{7}{4}$ is a critical point (numerator zero). Also checks that denominator is defined (nonzero) at $x = \frac{7}{4}$.

Correctly classifies $x = \frac{7}{4}$ as a relative maximum using a sign analysis of $f'$. Since $f'(x) = -\dfrac{4x-7}{(2x^2-7x+5)^2}$ and the denominator is always positive (when defined), the sign of $f'$ is determined by $-(4x-7)$. For $x < \frac{7}{4}$, $4x - 7 < 0$ so $f'(x) > 0$. For $x > \frac{7}{4}$, $4x - 7 > 0$ so $f'(x) < 0$. Since $f'$ changes from positive to negative at $x = \frac{7}{4}$, $f$ has a relative maximum there.

required_elements: $f'$ changes from positive to negative at $x = \frac{7}{4}$

common_traps: Confusing the sign analysis: for $x < 7/4$, the numerator $4x-7$ is negative, and the leading minus sign makes $f' > 0$, not $< 0$
Concluding relative minimum instead of relative maximum

parts.prompt: Using the identity $$\frac{1}{2x^2 - 7x + 5} = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{2}{2x-5} - \frac{1}{x-1}\right),$$ evaluate $\displaystyle\int_5^\infty f(x)\,dx$ or show that the integral diverges.

scoring_points.description: Sets up improper integral as a limit and applies the partial fraction identity

Evaluates the limit correctly and concludes the integral converges. As $b \to \infty$, $\dfrac{2b-5}{b-1} \to 2$, so $\displaystyle\lim_{b\to\infty}\frac{1}{3}\ln\frac{2b-5}{b-1} = \frac{1}{3}\ln 2$. Subtracting the lower bound value: $\frac{1}{3}\ln 2 - \frac{1}{3}\ln\frac{5}{4} = \frac{1}{3}\ln\frac{8}{5}$. The integral converges to $\dfrac{1}{3}\ln\dfrac{8}{5}$.
common_traps: Forgetting to subtract the lower-bound evaluation: the answer is $\frac{1}{3}\ln 2 - \frac{1}{3}\ln\frac{5}{4} = \frac{1}{3}\ln\frac{8}{5}$, not simply $\frac{1}{3}\ln 2$
Evaluating $\ln|2x-5|$ as $\frac{1}{2}\ln|2x-5|$ — the $u$-substitution factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is already absorbed by the coefficient $2$ in the partial fraction decomposition

parts.prompt: Determine whether the series $\displaystyle\sum_{n=5}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2n^2 - 7n + 5}$ converges or diverges. State the conditions of the test used for determining convergence or divergence.

scoring_points.description: Applies the Integral Test correctly: states the conditions (f is positive, continuous, and decreasing on $[5, \infty)$) and connects to the integral from part (c)

required_elements: $f(x)$ is positive, continuous, and decreasing for $x \geq 5$

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion: the series converges because the improper integral converges (from part (c))

required_elements: $\displaystyle\int_5^\infty f(x)\,dx$ converges, so the series converges by the Integral Test

scoring_points.correct_example: Since $f(x) > 0$, $f$ is continuous, and $f$ is decreasing for $x \geq 5$, the Integral Test applies. Because $\displaystyle\int_5^\infty f(x)\,dx$ converges (from part (c)), the series $\displaystyle\sum_{n=5}^\infty \frac{1}{2n^2 - 7n + 5}$ converges.
